Common Themes And Application:
Themes:
- God's supreme authority and power
- The transcendence of God
- Trust and faith in God
- Obedience to God

Life Application:
- Acknowledge and submit to God's authority in our lives
- Trust in God's strength and sovereignty
- Have faith in God's promises and guidance
- Obey God's commandments and follow His teachings
Symbols1Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses that recurs throughout the Bible is the symbol of God's majesty and strength. This symbol represents God's sovereignty and power over creation. It develops throughout the Bible as we see various passages describing God's majestic reign and his ability to control and calm the seas, symbolizing his authority and control over all aspects of the world.
Symbols2Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses is the cloak of Elijah. It represents the passing of prophetic authority and power from Elijah to Elisha. Throughout the Bible, the cloak symbolizes the anointing and presence of God's Spirit. In this passage, when Elisha strikes the water with Elijah's cloak, he is able to perform a miracle, demonstrating that the same Spirit that was with Elijah is now with him. This symbol recurs throughout the Bible to represent the empowering and equipping of individuals for God's work.
Symbols3Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses is the concept of "lifting up" or "exalting." This symbol recurs throughout the Bible and represents the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus. It signifies the fulfillment of God's plan for salvation and the revelation of Jesus as the Son of God. Examples of this symbol developing throughout the Bible include Moses lifting up the bronze serpent in Numbers 21:9 and Jesus being lifted up on the cross in John 12:32-33.
Summarize1: Psalm 93 praises God as the reigning King, clothed in majesty and strength. It declares that God's throne is established and eternal. The seas and waves proclaim God's might, and His statutes stand firm.
Summarize2: After Elijah is taken up to heaven, Elisha picks up his cloak, strikes the water with it, and crosses the Jordan River. The prophets from Jericho acknowledge that the spirit of Elijah now rests on Elisha and show him respect.
Summarize3: Jesus tells the Jews that they will look for Him but cannot go where He is going. They question if He will kill Himself. Jesus says they are from below, He is from above, and that they will die in their sins if they do not believe in Him. They ask who He is, and He says He has been telling them from the beginning. Jesus speaks about His Father, saying that when He is lifted up, they will know He is who He claims to be. He does not speak on His own, but only what the Father has taught Him. Many believed in Him as He spoke.
Behindinsights1: One interesting insight about this passage is that it highlights the power and majesty of the Lord. It portrays God as being robed in majesty, armed with strength, and established in His throne since eternity. Additionally, it describes the seas lifting up their voice and waves, emphasizing the might of the Lord. The passage also acknowledges that the Lord's statutes stand firm and that holiness adorns His house for endless days.
SocioSummary1: The socio-historical situation in which Psalm 93 was written is not explicitly stated in the passage. However, based on the themes and references within the psalm, it is believed to have been composed during a time of political instability or chaos. The psalm speaks of God's reign, majesty, and strength, suggesting that there may have been turmoil or uncertainty in the world around the writer. Psalm 93 is generally attributed to King David or another composer from that time, so it is possible it was written during David's reign or shortly afterward, a time marked by conflicts with neighboring nations. However, without more specific details, it is difficult to provide a definitive socio-historical analysis.
SocioSummary2: This passage is set in ancient Israel when the country was divided into two kingdoms. It focuses on Elisha, the successor of Elijah, as he crosses the Jordan River. The prophets from Jericho acknowledge Elisha as the new leader, recognizing that the spirit of Elijah now rests upon him.
SocioSummary3: The passage from John 8:21-30 was written during a time of Roman occupation in Judea, with tensions between Jewish people and the Roman authorities. Jewish religious leaders were looking for a Messiah to deliver them from Roman rule, and there were different sects with varying beliefs about the Messiah. The passage reflects a dispute between Jesus and the Jewish leaders about his identity and where he is going. Understanding this socio-historical context helps to grasp the theological and historical significance of the dialogue in this passage.

Scene1: The scene in the movie "The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch, and the Wardrobe" where Aslan, the majestic lion who represents God, is seen standing on a cliff overlooking the ocean. The waves crash against the rocks and the thunderous sound fills the surroundings, symbolizing the might and power of God as described in Psalm 93.
Scene2: The scene from the movie "The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe" where Aslan uses his power to divide the frozen river is similar to this passage. Just like Elisha using Elijah's cloak to part the Jordan river, Aslan uses his divine power to split the ice, allowing the characters to cross to safety. The show of power and the recognition of Elisha's authority by the prophets parallels the awe and reverence shown to Aslan in the movie.
Scene3: The scene in the movie "The Matrix" where Neo is being interrogated by Agent Smith is similar to this passage. Neo is questioned about his identity and purpose, just as Jesus is questioned about who he is. Both characters face disbelief and lack of understanding from those interrogating them. Additionally, Neo's line "I know what I'm talking about, cause I've been down there" parallels Jesus' assertion that he is not of this world.

emily1:
In robes of majesty, the Lord does reign,
His strength and firmness, the world maintains.
Established long ago, His timeless throne,
From all eternity, His power is known.

The seas, they lift their voices high,
With pounding waves that touch the sky.
Yet mightier still, the Lord's great might,
Above the breakers, His power takes flight.

His statutes, firm and unswayed they stand,
In holiness, His house is grand.
Through endless days, His presence graces,
A reminder of His everlasting traces.
emily2:
In riversong of Jordan's flow,
Elijah's cloak did Elisha sow.
With strength of faith, he struck the tide,
Dividing water far and wide.

"Where now is the Lord, mighty and just?"
He questioned with unwavering trust.
The waters parted, a path was made,
And Elisha crossed, unafraid.

The prophets of Jericho's sacred ground,
Witnessed the spirit, profound and profound.
In humble awe, they bowed their heads,
Elijah's mantle now on Elisha, it spreads.

This scripture speaks of faith's remarkable might,
By the river's edge, our fears take flight.
With steadfast trust, we question and find,
Miracles unfold when God's presence binds.

In life's swirling currents, we may ask,
Where does God dwell in our burdensome task?
But in each trial, His strength will abide,
For the spirit of Elijah still resides.
emily3:
In the stillness of the night,
Jesus spoke, his words took flight.
"I go away, you'll seek in vain,
In sin you'll die, my love in pain."

The Jews, perplexed, they questioned why,
Would he, despairing, choose to die?
But Jesus, steadfast, would not yield,
He claimed, "From above, my truth revealed."

"You are of Earth, I come from high,
Believe in me, or else you'll die.
I am he," he said once more,
His purpose, sure, his heart, outpour.

And yet, they asked, "Who are you?"
To which he answered, "You've always knew.
Words I speak, judgment rings,
Sent from above on heavenly wings."

Though they didn't grasp the Father's role,
When he's exalted, truth will unroll.
For in unity, they're not apart,
Alone, he speaks the Father's heart.

In awe, many believed his claim,
His words of love, his sacred name.
With faith, they journeyed to his side,
Where truth and grace forever reside.
Snoop1:
Yo, check it out, Snoop D-O-double G droppin' some rhymes,
 'Bout our Lord, so listen close, don't waste no time.

Poundin' waves, makin' noise, it's their choice.

But ain't nothin' can compare to the thunderous sound,

He's mighty, y'all, there ain't no denyin',

In his holy house, righteousness all day long.

Snoop2:
Yo, listen up, I got a story to flow,
'Bout a cat named Elisha, let me break it down low.
He had that cloak from Elijah, feelin' the power,
Walked to the river Jordan, ready to devour.

He struck the water, like "Where's the Most High?"
The waves, they split, like the sky in the night sky.
To the right, to the left, he passed right on through,
Elisha got that faith, ain't nothin' he can't do.

The prophets from Jericho, eyes wide, amazed,
They saw that Elijah's spirit on Elisha blazed.
They came, they bowed, respect on the ground,
Recognizin' his greatness, that's how it went down.
Snoop3:
Yo, listen up, I got a story to tell,
It's about Jesus, and the Jews, can you hear the bell?
He said, "I'm goin' away, you'll search for me,
But you'll die in sin, where I go, you can't be."

The Jews wondered, "Is he talkin' 'bout suicide?"
"Why he sayin', 'Where I'm goin', you can't ride?'"
Jesus said, "Y'all from below, I'm from above,
In this world, you dwell, I bring divine love."

He warned them, "You'll die in sins, that's true,
Only by believin' I am He, you'll make it through."
They asked, "Who are you? Tell us, make it clear."
He said, "Been tellin' y'all, don't you hear?"

"I got a lotta things to say, judgment awaits,
But the one who sent me, he never hesitates.
I speak what I've heard, the truth I unfurl,
Spreadin' the word from the most high in this world."

They couldn't grasp, he spoke 'bout his Father above,
So Jesus told 'em, "When I'm raised up, you'll feel the love.
Then you'll know, I am He, not actin' alone,
Only speakin' what my Father has shown."

"The one who sent me, he's by my side,
Never alone, his presence is my guide.
I always please him, in all that I do,
And as I spoke, many believed, knew it was true."
moviesummary1: Psalm 93 is reflected in various works of literature and film, such as "The Lord of the Rings" by J.R.R. Tolkien, "The Chronicles of Narnia" by C.S. Lewis, the movie "Noah," "Thor," and "Excalibur." These works explore themes of power, majesty, the establishment of order, and the triumph of good over evil.
moviesummary2: These five stories all feature a theme of passing power from one hero to another. Whether it is the inheritance of authority and responsibility or the passing on of knowledge and abilities, these narratives explore the idea of a new generation stepping up to carry on the legacy of their predecessors.
moviesummary3: The passage from John 8:21-30 revolves around Jesus asserting his divinity and challenging the disbelief of the Jews. Similar themes and storylines can be found in novels and movies like The Da Vinci Code, Life of Pi, The Matrix trilogy, The Gospel According to Jesus Christ, and Contact.

applicationq1: How does recognizing the power and majesty of God in creation impact your perspective on the difficulties and challenges you face in life?
applicationq2: How can we cultivate a faith that enables us to step into the calling and anointing that God has placed upon us, just as Elisha did when he picked up Elijah's cloak and crossed the Jordan River?
applicationq3: How does Jesus' statement "If you do not believe that I am he, you will indeed die in your sins" challenge us to examine our own beliefs about who Jesus is?
Enneagram1:
Type 1 (The Perfectionist):
- Finds comfort in the idea of a firm and secure world established by God.
- Appreciates the mention of God's statutes, finding reassurance in the presence of rules and order.
- May feel a sense of duty to follow God's commandments and uphold holiness in their own life.

Type 4 (The Individualist):
- Identifies with the imagery of the seas lifting up their voice and pounding waves, reflecting their own intense emotions.
- Symbolizes the overwhelming and powerful nature of God, which may resonate with their desire for depth and significance.
- Draws inspiration from the mention of holiness, seeking to create a unique and meaningful existence that reflects their authentic self.
Enneagram2:
Type 1 (Perfectionist):

- Analyzes the passage from a theological perspective, seeking to understand the symbolism and significance of Elisha's actions
- Reflects on the obedience and faithfulness displayed by Elisha, acknowledging the divine power at work
- Draws lessons on the importance of spiritual succession and the impact of genuine spiritual leadership

Type 7 (Enthusiast):

- Excitedly imagines the scene, envisioning the dramatic moment when Elisha strikes the water, and it miraculously divides
- Gets caught up in the awe and wonder of the supernatural events, experiencing a sense of joy and adventure
- Thinks about the reactions of the company of prophets and pictures the celebration and excitement that followed Elisha's successful crossing of the Jordan river
Enneagram3:
Type 5: The Investigator

- Questions the meaning behind Jesus' statement about going away and not being able to follow
- Searches for logical explanations for Jesus' words
- Interested in understanding Jesus' identity and the concept of dying in sins
- Analyzes the connection between Jesus and the Father, trying to piece together the truth

Type 9: The Peacemaker

- Feels a sense of confusion and tries to find a peaceful resolution among the disputing Jews and Jesus
- Tends to avoid getting too caught up in debates or conflicts
- Focuses on the aspects of Jesus' words that promote unity and doing what pleases God
- Draws comfort from Jesus' assurance that he is not alone and tries to bring others to believe in him
Myersbriggs1:
- An ENFP might be inspired by the majestic imagery in the passage and feel a sense of awe and wonder. They might see it as a reminder of the beauty and power of the world and the divine.
- An ISTJ might appreciate the sense of stability and order conveyed in the passage. They might focus on the established nature of the world and the firmness of the Lord's statutes, finding comfort in the idea of a reliable and unchanging foundation.
Myersbriggs2:
- An INTJ might approach this passage from a logical perspective, focusing on the symbolism of the cloak and the demonstration of Elisha's abilities. They might analyze the supernatural act of dividing the water with the cloak, questioning the scientific principles behind it.
- An ENFJ might approach this passage from an emotional and spiritual perspective, seeing it as a profound moment of divine intervention. They might appreciate the symbolism of the passing of the cloak and empathize with the awe and reverence of the company of prophets, finding inspiration in their act of bowing before Elisha.
Myersbriggs3:
- An INFJ (Introverted, Intuitive, Feeling, Judging) might resonate with the deeper spiritual message in this passage and find comfort in Jesus' words about being from above and not of this world, connecting it to their pursuit of inner spirituality and the desire to understand the higher meaning in life.
- An ESTP (Extraverted, Sensing, Thinking, Perceiving) might focus more on the confusion and skepticism of the Jews, questioning Jesus' intentions and wanting to know the practical implications of his claims. They might be intrigued by Jesus' assertiveness and confidence in his purpose.
psychology1:
Theme: The Power and Stability of God

Contemporary psychology research has shown that belief in a higher power can have positive effects on mental health and well-being. Studies have found that individuals who have a strong belief in God or a higher power show greater resilience, lower levels of stress and anxiety, and more positive coping mechanisms during challenging times. This suggests that the perception of a powerful and stable God can provide individuals with a sense of security and support, contributing to their overall psychological well-being. (Source: Ano, G. G., & Vasconcelles, E. B. (2005). Religious coping and psychological adjustment to stress: A meta-analysis. Journal of Clinical Psychology, 61(4), 461-480.)
psychology2: One theme from this text is the transfer of power and authority from one person to another. This theme can be related to findings in contemporary psychology research on leadership and social influence. Research has shown that individuals who are perceived as having the qualities and abilities of a leader can influence others and gain their respect and admiration, effectively transferring power and authority from one person to another (e.g., Hogg, van Knippenberg, & Rast, 2012).
psychology3:
One theme from this text is the idea of belief and understanding. Jesus emphasizes that those who do not believe that he is who he claims to be will die in their sins. Similarly, in contemporary psychology research, belief and understanding play a crucial role in psychological well-being and resilience. For example, studies show that having a sense of purpose and beliefs about the meaning of life can contribute to overall mental health and subjective well-being (Ryff & Singer, 2008; King et al., 2006). Furthermore, developing a deep understanding of one's emotions and thoughts through practices like mindfulness can lead to greater emotional regulation and psychological resilience (Kabat-Zinn, 2005; Siegel, 2007). Thus, the theme of belief and understanding highlighted in the text aligns with findings in contemporary psychology research. 

References:
Kabat-Zinn, J. (2005). Wherever you go, there you are: Mindfulness meditation in everyday life. Hachette UK.
King, L. A., Hicks, J. A., Krull, J. L., & Del Gaiso, A. K. (2006). Positive affect and the experience of meaning in life. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 90(1), 179-196.
Ryff, C. D., & Singer, B. H. (2008). Know thyself and become what you are: A eudaimonic approach to psychological well-being. Journal of Happiness Studies, 9(1), 13-39.
Siegel, D. J. (2007). The mindful brain: Reflection and attunement in the cultivation of well-being. WW Norton & Company.
biology1:
Theme: The power and majesty of God

Scientific finding: The ability of certain plants to survive in extreme environments, such as hot deserts or freezing tundras, due to their unique adaptations.

Research sources:
- Brooker, R. J., et al. (2014). Plant adaptation to extreme environments. In Biology (4th ed., pp. 898-901). McGraw-Hill Education.
- Lázaro-Nogal, A., et al. (2015). Plant traits determine the phylogenetic structure of late-successional communities in a dry ecosystem. Plant Ecology, 216(1), 59-71.
biology2:
Theme: Succession of Power

In the field of biology, the theme of succession of power can be observed in the process of cellular differentiation. This is the transformation of unspecialized cells into specialized cell types with specific functions. Just as Elisha received the power and spirit of Elijah, stem cells can differentiate into various cell types, acquiring specific roles within an organism. This succession of power ensures the development and functioning of different tissues and organs in multicellular organisms.

Research sources: 

1. Gupta, P., & Srivastava, S. (2018). Strategies for Stem Cell Production and Effect of Stem Cells on Succession of Plant Stressed Cells. Molecular biology reports, 45(6), 2503--2515. doi: 10.1007/s11033-018-4388-9

2. Maddalena, P., & Campbell, P. H. (2015). The Power of Pluripotency: Lessons from Stem Cells. Molecular reproduction and development, 82(3), 145--147. doi: 10.1002/mrd.22473
biology3:
Theme: Identity and Belief

Scientific Finding: The effect of belief on immune system functioning

Researchers have found that belief and identity can have a significant impact on immune system functioning. In a study published in the Journal of Applied Biobehavioral Research, participants who strongly identified themselves with a social group exhibited enhanced immune responses. This effect, known as the "social identity boost," was observed through increased production of antibodies and enhanced immune cell activity. These findings highlight the power of belief and identity in influencing physiological responses, including the functioning of the immune system. (Source: Cruwys, T., Haslam, S. A., Dingle, G. A., Jetten, J., Hornsey, M. J., Chong, E., & Oei, T. P. (2014). Feeling connected again: Interventions that increase social identification reduce depression symptoms in community and clinical settings. Journal of Affective Disorders, 159, 139-146.)
